briefing.headline: Capital reform turns lighter-touch even as the AI-governance clock counts down to August enforcement.
body: The prudential direction of travel has reversed. The latest cross-agency capital proposals lower requirements overall and rescind the more onerous 2023 framework, improving the economics of traditional lending and, on the margin, pulling some activity back toward the regulated balance sheet. Funding markets read the shift as supportive, with spreads on financial paper holding firm into quarter-end.

Supervisory attention, however, is moving the opposite way. With AI Act enforcement powers and general-purpose-AI duties taking effect on 2 August, the documentation bar for model-assisted decisions is rising sharply, while third-party concentration and operational-incident readiness now sit at the centre of the examination cycle across both jurisdictions.

watching.title: AI-governance clock
watching.note: Five weeks to AI Act enforcement; evidence for model-assisted decisions must be examination-ready.

watching.title: Capital reform direction
watching.note: Lighter requirements may repatriate activity to banks; watch the finalisation drafting.

watching.title: Critical third-party oversight
watching.note: First CTPP designations and tighter UK–EU coordination raise the bar on concentration.

reg.title: AI Act — GPAI duties & enforcement begin
reg.detail: Commission enforcement powers and transparency obligations for general-purpose AI providers take effect, with fines up to 7% of global turnover.

reg.title: Capital framework re-proposal — finalisation phase
reg.detail: With the comment window closed on 18 June, agencies begin drafting final rules that lower requirements versus the rescinded 2023 framework.

reg.title: DORA — Critical Third-Party Provider designations
reg.detail: Supervisors progress the first CTPP designations off the register of information, sharpening concentration-risk oversight.

reg.title: Operational-incident & third-party reporting — build phase
reg.detail: Firms scope systems for the new serious-incident and material-arrangement regime ahead of the 18 March 2027 go-live.

reg.title: Consumer Duty — annual board attestation
reg.detail: Boards prepare the next annual assessment of good outcomes across the full product set.

reg.title: Banking supervisory priorities — resilience & ICT
reg.detail: The new supervisory cycle centres on operational resilience, ICT capability and geopolitical risk.

controls.title: Privileged-access recertification lapsed
controls.detail: Quarterly attestation for elevated accounts missed across several business units; standing access went unreviewed.

controls.title: Model-decision documentation gap
controls.detail: The evidence trail for model-assisted credit and surveillance decisions fell short of the rising supervisory bar.

controls.title: Cloud concentration without a tested exit
controls.detail: Multiple critical services mapped to a single provider region with no exercised exit or failover plan.

controls.title: Incident-reporting readiness gap
controls.detail: Detection-to-report timelines would miss the incoming serious-incident clock under the new regime.

controls.title: Trade-surveillance model drift
controls.detail: Alert thresholds calibrated to stale volatility produced a measurable false-negative gap.

controls.title: KYC refresh backlog — high-risk tier
controls.detail: Periodic-review cadence slipped beyond policy for the highest-risk customer segment.

board.headline: The week resilience stopped being a cost centre and started setting the agenda.
body: Three observations for the board. First, the prudential floor is being lowered — the latest capital proposals reduce requirements rather than add to them — but the relief comes with a clear condition: supervisors expect that headroom to be matched by demonstrable operational strength.

Second, the supervisory bar for explaining AI-assisted decisions has risen sharply with enforcement weeks away, and third, third-party concentration is now a board-level resilience question rather than a procurement footnote, with the first critical-provider designations underway.
decisions: Approve the AI-decisioning documentation uplift ahead of the 2 August enforcement date.
Endorse a tested exit and failover plan for the most concentrated critical third-party dependency.
Note the build plan for the incoming operational-incident reporting regime and confirm second-line ownership.
board.statNote: the date EU AI Act enforcement powers and GPAI transparency duties take effect.

essay.title: As capital rules loosen, resilience becomes the binding constraint.
essay.lead: For the better part of a decade, the prudential conversation had a single currency: capital. How much to hold, against which assets, under whose model. This year the direction of travel reversed — the latest proposals lighten the load rather than add to it — and in doing so they quietly moved the binding constraint somewhere else.
bodyTop: That constraint is now operational. Supervisors who once asked whether an institution could absorb a loss are increasingly asking whether it can keep running — through a failed vendor, a drifting model, or a cyber event measured in hours rather than quarters. Capital answers the first question; it says almost nothing about the second.
essay.pullquote: Capital tells you whether a firm can survive a loss. Resilience tells you whether it can survive a Tuesday.
bodyBottom: This is why the regulatory centre of gravity has shifted to third-party concentration, incident reporting and the governance of automated decisions. These are not capital questions. They are questions about whether an institution understands its own dependencies well enough to defend them — and can prove it on demand, not once a year.

The firms that adapt fastest will treat resilience the way they once treated capital: as a number managed continuously, not a posture asserted annually. The prudential floor is being lowered. The operational bar is not.
